behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: A pure unit test of sorting logic can miss real failures in file discovery + parsing + execution wiring (e.g., glob order, filename parsing edge cases, runner-to-DB sequencing). The minimum reliable boundary is runner + real migrations directory + real PostgreSQL state checks.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp `migrations/` dir with out-of-order filenames (e.g., `002_b.sql`, `010_c.sql`, `001_a.sql`) where each file inserts its version into a test table. Run the migration runner once against a real test Postgres DB, then assert inserted rows show execution order `1,2,10` and `_migrations` records the same order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Idempotency depends on persisted DB metadata (`_migrations`) and re-run behavior against real DB state. In-process mocks can hide schema/transaction/query mismatches that cause accidental re-execution.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real test Postgres DB and a migration that performs a non-idempotent side effect (e.g., insert fixed row). Run runner twice. Assert side effect appears only once and `_migrations` contains one record for that migration.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Rollback correctness is primarily DB transaction semantics plus runner control flow. A mocked DB can falsely pass rollback behavior. Real subprocess/OS behavior is not required for this failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare three migrations: first inserts row A, second starts with insert row B then executes invalid SQL, third inserts row C. Run once against real Postgres. Assert: row A exists, row B does not (rolled back), row C does not (runner stopped), and only first migration is recorded in `_migrations`.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: The requirement explicitly targets separate processes and concurrent startup timing. In-process tests or mocks can miss process-level race conditions, lock timing, and transaction interleavings that only appear with real subprocess execution.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two OS processes of the migration runner concurrently against the same real Postgres DB and identical migrations. Wait for both to finish. Assert each migration’s side effect appears exactly once, `_migrations` has one record per migration (no duplicates), and both processes exit without corrupting state.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This is initialization ordering across filesystem discovery, DB schema bootstrap, and migration execution. Mocking DB setup can hide real DDL/order failures; full subprocess orchestration is not strictly necessary.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a fresh Postgres database containing no `_migrations` table. Include at least one migration file that performs a simple DML/DDL action. Run runner once and assert `_migrations` exists and contains the applied migration record; also verify migration succeeded, demonstrating bootstrap happened before migration bookkeeping writes.
